title: Setup
text:
To follow along with the course exercises, you’ll need R, Quarto, a code editor that supports R, and a few R packages.
While the workshop isn’t centered around Quarto, the exercises and examples will all be based on Quarto documents so that text and code and output can all be included together, making a nicer resource for future you.

title: Setup
text:
Recommended setup options
There are two ways to get set up for the workshop:

Local installation
Posit Cloud

While Posit Cloud lets you run RStudio directly in your browser and makes it so you don’t need to install anything on your computer (convenient!), I highly recommend installing R and RStudio/Positron on your computer. The Bayesian models we’ll run won’t run well on Posit Cloud, which by default only has once CPU core and limited RAM.

Option 1: Local installation (most recommended)
Choose one editor and follow the steps:

RStudioPositron



Download and install R: https://cloud.r-project.org/

Make sure R is version 4.5+

Download and install RStudio: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop

Make sure RStudio is version 2026.04+

Quarto comes pre-installed with RStudio

Make sure Quarto is version 1.9+; run quarto --version in RStudio’s terminal to confirm





Download and install R: https://cloud.r-project.org/

Make sure R is version 4.5+

Download and install Positron: https://positron.posit.co/download.html
Download and install Quarto: https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/

Make sure Quarto is version 1.9+







Option 2: Posit Cloud (RStudio in the cloud)

Create a free account at https://posit.cloud/
Join the Posit Cloud workspace: https://posit.cloud/spaces/791899/join?access_code=irmcMZ3L9XLiffB-iP2a7RhtqsH8MZ78JVcgXndk
Click “Yes” to join the space
Click Content and select the mastering-conjoint-analysis-materials_2026-06 project
To save a copy of the project to your workspace, click “Save a permanent copy”

title: Setup
text:
Accessing the course materials
If you are using Posit Cloud option, the files will be included.
If you are using RStudio or Positron locally:

If you don’t use Git/GitHubIf you use Git/GitHub



Go to the GitHub repository for the course materials:

https://github.com/andrewheiss/mastering-conjoint-analysis-materials_2026-06

Click the green “Code” button → “Download ZIP”.
Unzip the folder and open it in your chosen editor.




Course materials are available here:

https://github.com/andrewheiss/mastering-conjoint-analysis-materials_2026-06

Clone the repository with Git:
git clone https://github.com/andrewheiss/mastering-conjoint-analysis-materials_2026-06.git

title: Setup
text:
Install the necessary R packages
If you are using Posit Cloud, the packages will be pre-installed (kind of! there won’t be any Bayesian stuff).
If you are using RStudio or Positron locally, install all the packages we’ll use with:
install.packages(
  c(
    "tidyverse", 
    "patchwork", 
    "marginaleffects", 
    "easystats", 
    "cbcTools", 
    "mclogit", 
    "lme4", 
    "logitr", 
    "remotes", 
    "pak", 
    "brms", 
    "rstan", 
    "rstanarm", 
    "collapse", 
    "shiny", 
    "bslib", 
    "bsicons", 
    "tidybayes", 
    "ggdist", 
    "modelsummary", 
    "ggforce", 
    "ggh4x", 
    "broom", 
    "broom.mixed"
  )
)

pak::pak("surveydown-dev/surveydown")

title: Setup
text:
Install {CmdStanR}
We’ll use Stan through {brms} to run the Bayesian models. You should have already installed {brms} in the install.packages() command above.






NoteRaw Stan



If you’ve never worked with Stan, don’t worry! You won’t have to write a single line of Stan code in this workshop. {brms} translates familiar R model syntax into Stan code, runs the MCMC sampler, and bring the results back into R, all without you needing to see what’s happening. It’s magical.


Stan has two R-based interfaces for communicating between the two programs: RStan and CmdStanR. {brms} works with both backends. I recommend installing CmdStan. CmdStan is newer and has more modern features (like speeding up sampling with multi-threading), and it works nicely with the more complex multinomial models we’ll be running.
The {CmdStanR} package helps you install CmdStan on your computer and provides the actual interface to bridge Stan and R. It is not on CRAN because it requires other binary software for connecting to Stan, so you have to install it a little differently:

Install the {CmdStanR} package. Use this command in R to install the package from Stan’s R-Universe repository:
install.packages("cmdstanr", repos = c('https://stan-dev.r-universe.dev', getOption("repos")))
Install a C++ toolchain. Stan models need to be compiled to binary programs to run. See here for complete instructions for installing the required tools. In short:

On Windows, download and install Rtools45: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
On macOS, install Xcode’s command line tools by running this in a terminal:
xcode-select --install
On Linux, install g++ and make. make is typically pre-installed, and g++ is generally also installed. If not, run something like this to install them (this assumes a Debian-based distribution like Ubuntu; adapt the command as necessary for your distribution):
sudo apt install g++ make

Make sure the C++ toolchain is working. In R, run this in your console to make sure everything’s set up correctly:
cmdstanr::check_cmdstan_toolchain()
Install CmdStan. Finally, you need to compile and install CmdStan. You can do that with {cmdstanr} in R. Run this in your console:
cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()
Make sure everything works. Run this in R to make sure everything’s working correctly:
library(brms)

model <- brm(
  bf(mpg ~ disp + am),
  data = mtcars,
  family = gaussian(),
  backend = "cmdstan"
)
You should see something like this:
model
#>  Family: gaussian 
#>   Links: mu = identity 
#> Formula: mpg ~ disp + am 
#>    Data: mtcars (Number of observations: 32) 
#>   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
#>          total post-warmup draws = 4000
#> 
#> Regression Coefficients:
#>           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
#> Intercept    27.81      1.90    24.08    31.52 1.00     2910     2508
#> disp         -0.04      0.01    -0.05    -0.03 1.00     3233     2894
#> am            1.87      1.48    -1.02     4.78 1.00     3117     2897
#> 
#> Further Distributional Parameters:
#>       Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
#> sigma     3.33      0.47     2.56     4.38 1.00     3026     2881
#> 
#> Draws were sampled using sample(hmc). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
#> and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
#> scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
About me


Andrew Heiss

 andrewheiss.com  @andrew.heiss.phd
 @andrewheiss   andrewheiss


Assistant professor of public policy international relations, Georgia State University Georgetown University in Qatar
Data visualization, statistics, and causal inference

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Meeting you where you are



This course is designed for someone who:

Is familiar with R (and ideally tidyverse)
Has some experience with statistical modeling with linear models
Maybe has an idea for a conjoint experiment






You’ll learn:

What conjoint analysis is
How to measure causal effects and explore “product” and “consumer” preferences
How to design and run your own conjoint experiments

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Course structure


My turn

Lecture segments
Feel free to just watch, take notes, browse docs, or tinker around with the code
Lots of live-coding! Most content lives in Quarto files


Your turn

Exercises for you to do
Work on your own or with others

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Bring your own ideas!

The best way to learn is by building something
I’ve given you playground code, but it’s fairly basic. This is by design!
You’ll have time during the “Your turn”s to to play around and experiment. Try to use these new principles and skills to create something!

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Getting help




Use Zoom reactions

 =“I’m stuck and need help!”
 =“I finished the exercise”

Ask longer, more detailed questions in Slack

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Your turn
Introduce yourself:

Name and professional affiliation
On a scale of 1–10, how well do you know…

R?
Tidyverse?
Statistical models like OLS, multinomial logit, and Bayesian methods?
Conjoint analysis?

What do you hope to get out of this course?

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
The problem of preferences
How do you measure what people really want?

Direct questions: “Do you care about the environment?” → Everyone says yes
Stated vs. revealed preference: What people say ≠ what they do
Single attributes: “Is healthcare important?” Of course! But how important vs. cost? Do they interact?
Ranking: People rank things differently depending on context


People make trade-offs. Survey experiments that don’t force trade-offs can give misleading answers.

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Ice cream!
It’s hot outside and you want to buy some ice cream. You go to your local ice cream shop and find a menu with all these possibilities:

Vanilla, chocolate, or strawberry
Served in a bowl or served in a waffle cone
No extra toppings, topped with nuts, or topped with sprinkles
Priced at $3, $4, or $5

Which kind do you want? Why?

No ice cream maximizes everyone’s happiness.
There are too many trade-offs

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
What is conjoint analysis?

Conjoint analysis is a family of survey-based techniques where respondents evaluate or choose among hypothetical profiles described by multiple attributes, revealing their preferences through the trade-offs they make.


conjoint = considered jointly
Respondents evaluate attributes together (as bundles), not separately
Use statistics to uncover preferences from these bundles

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text: andhs.co/icecream

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Psychology (1960s)



Luce, R. Duncan, and John W. Tukey. 1964. “Simultaneous Conjoint Measurement: A New Type of Fundamental Measurement.” Journal of Mathematical Psychology 1 (1): 1–27. https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-2496(64)90015-X.


 
Originally developed to measure preferences of sets of features

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Marketing research (1970s)



Green, Paul E., and Vithala R. Rao. 1971. “Conjoint Measurement for Quantifying Judgmental Data.” Journal of Marketing Research 8 (3): 355. https://doi.org/10.2307/3149575.


Adapted to answer marketing questions:

How should we price our product?
Which features should we invest in?
What product will maximize market share?

Uses: consumer electronics, airlines, credit cards, pharmaceuticals, automobiles

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Apple vs. Samsung (2012)

Apple sued Samsung for patent infringement: copied features like sliding to unlock, search, autocorrect, pinch-to-zoom, etc.
Apple hired MIT marketing professor John Hauser to measure value of those features
Conjoint experiment analyzed with a hierarchical Bayesian model
Consumers would be willing to spend between $32–$102 extra for these features
These results (and other bits of evidence) scaled up to $2 billion in damages awarded to Apple

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Causal inference (2010s)



Hainmueller, Jens, Daniel J. Hopkins, and Teppei Yamamoto. 2014. “Causal Inference in Conjoint Analysis: Understanding Multidimensional Choices via Stated Preference Experiments.” Political Analysis 22 (1): 1–30. https://doi.org/10.1093/pan/mpt024.


Instead of analyzing a whole constellation of attributes, look at specific levers individually.


Conjoints are like RCTs, just fancier.
Since treatment assignment is random, we can find the effect on the selection of probability (or favorability) when changing an attribute.
Uses: Measure effects of political candidate characteristics, issue salience, organization characteristics

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Preferences
Marketing

How much do people value the overall constellation of features?
What product will sell best?
What are people willing to pay for this feature?
Estimands: utilities, attribute importance, willingness to pay, market shares and simulations


Causal inference
Social science

Does feature X make people more or less likely to choose a “product”?
What is the average causal effect of a specific feature?
Estimands: average marginal component effect (AMCE), marginal means, average feature choice probability (AFCP)

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text:
Same data and same models,
completely different estimands

title: Introduction toconjoint analysis
text: Crossing traditions!

text:
Crossing traditions!








Chaudhry, Suparna, Marc Dotson, and Andrew Heiss. 2021. “Who Cares about Crackdowns? Exploring the Role of Trust in Individual Philanthropy.” Global Policy 12 (S5): 45–58. https://doi.org/10.1111/1758-5899.12984.








Chaudhry, Suparna, Marc Dotson, and Andrew Heiss. 2025. “Navigating Hostility: The Effect of Nonprofit Transparency and Accountability on Donor Preferences in the Face of Shrinking Civic Space.” Nonprofit and Voluntary Sector Quarterly, 1–27. https://doi.org/10.1177/08997640251348654.

title: Causal inferencewith conjoints
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(marginaleffects)
library(parameters)

penguins <- penguins |> drop_na(sex)

model1 <- lm(body_mass ~ flipper_len, data = penguins)
model2 <- lm(body_mass ~ flipper_len + species, data = penguins)


 

model_parameters(model1)

Parameter   | Coefficient |     SE |               95% CI | t(331) |      p
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept) |    -5872.09 | 310.29 | [-6482.47, -5261.71] | -18.92 | < .001
flipper len |       50.15 |   1.54 | [   47.12,    53.18] |  32.56 | < .001




 

model_parameters(model2)

Parameter           | Coefficient |     SE |               95% CI | t(329) |      p
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept)         |    -4013.18 | 586.25 | [-5166.44, -2859.92] |  -6.85 | < .001
flipper len         |       40.61 |   3.08 | [   34.55,    46.66] |  13.19 | < .001
species [Chinstrap] |     -205.38 |  57.57 | [ -318.62,   -92.13] |  -3.57 | < .001
species [Gentoo]    |      284.52 |  95.43 | [   96.79,   472.25] |   2.98 | 0.003

title: Causal inferencewith conjoints
text:
{marginaleffects}

Quit working with raw coefficients!


Arel-Bundock, Vincent, Noah Greifer, and Andrew Heiss. 2024. “How to Interpret Statistical Models Using marginaleffects for R and Python.” Journal of Statistical Software 111 (9): 1–32. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v111.i09.
Arel-Bundock, Vincent. 2026. Model to Meaning: How to Interpret Statistical Models with R and Python. A Chapman & Hall Book. CRC Press, Taylor & Francis Group. https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003560333, https://marginaleffects.com/

title: Causal inferencewith conjoints
text:
Quantity: What is the quantity of interest? Do we want to report a prediction or a function of predictions (average, difference, ratio, derivative, etc.)?
Grid: What predictor values are we interested in? Do we want to report estimates for the units in our dataset, or for hypothetical or representative individuals?
Aggregation: Do we report estimates for every observation in the grid or a global summary?
Uncertainty: How do we quantify uncertainty about our estimates?
Test: Which (non-)linear hypothesis or equivalence tests do we conduct?

title: Causal inferencewith conjoints
text: plot_predictions(model3, condition = c("flipper_len", "species"))

title: Causal inferencewith conjoints
text:
OLS with categorical predictors

model <- lm(body_mass ~ species + sex, data = penguins)

title: Causal inferencewith conjoints
text:
Predictions across balanced grid

grid <- datagrid(model = model, species = unique, sex = unique)

preds <- predictions(model, grid)

 



preds |> 
  as_tibble() |> 
  select(species, sex, estimate)

title: Mastering Conjoint Analysis
text:
Overview
Conjoint experiments have become a popular tool in both social science and marketing research for understanding how people make decisions and what attributes matter most in their choices. Originally developed by marketers to measure consumer preferences for new products, conjoint analysis has expanded into political science, economics, sociology, and other fields as a powerful method for testing multiple causal effects simultaneously and eliciting respondent preferences. With a single well-designed survey, you can answer complex research questions about decision-making that would require dozens of traditional experiments.
In this course, you’ll learn the theory behind forced choice conjoint experiments, how to design studies that answer your research questions, how to program and field these survey experiments in online platforms, and how to analyze and visualize the results using open source tools like R, Quarto, {marginaleffects}, and {ggplot2}. You’ll explore key causal estimands like average marginal component effects (AMCEs) and marginal means (MMs), as well as preference-focused measures like part-worth utilities and willingness-to-pay. By the end of the course, you’ll be able to design and field your own conjoint experiments, analyze the results with appropriate methods, and communicate your findings effectively with publication-ready tables and figures.

title: Mastering Conjoint Analysis
text:
Instructor
Andrew Heiss is an assistant professor in the Department of Public Management and Policy at the Andrew Young School of Policy Studies at Georgia State University. His research explores human rights and international nonprofit management, and focuses on authoritarian regulation of civil society and INGO responses to administrative crackdown. Heiss also studies and teaches quantitative research methods, causal inference, data visualization, and Bayesian analysis. He is an RStudio Certified Instructor whose work has appeared in journals such as the Journal of Politics, Nonprofit and Voluntary Sector Quarterly, the Journal of Statistical Software, the Journal of Human Rights, and International Interactions.
